questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the efficiency equation using energy transfers.

questions.stem: Which equation can be used to calculate the efficiency of a device?
questions.question: Which equation can be used to calculate the efficiency of a device?
options: efficiency = useful output energy transfer ÷ total input energy transfer
efficiency = total input energy transfer ÷ useful output energy transfer
efficiency = wasted energy transfer ÷ total input energy transfer
efficiency = useful output power × total input power
questions.correctAnswer: efficiency = useful output energy transfer ÷ total input energy transfer
questions.explanation: Efficiency compares the useful output with the total input. The ratio can be written as a decimal or multiplied by 100 for a percentage.

distractorRationales.text: efficiency = total input energy transfer ÷ useful output energy transfer
efficiency = wasted energy transfer ÷ total input energy transfer
efficiency = useful output power × total input power

questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the efficiency equation using energy transfers.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate efficiency as a percentage from energy values.

questions.stem: A motor transfers 80 J usefully from 200 J supplied. What is its efficiency?
questions.question: A motor transfers 80 J usefully from 200 J supplied. What is its efficiency?
questions.explanation: Efficiency = useful output ÷ total input = 80 ÷ 200 = 0.40 = 40%.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate efficiency as a percentage from energy values.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Convert decimal efficiency into a percentage.

questions.stem: A machine has an efficiency of 0.65. What is this efficiency as a percentage?
questions.question: A machine has an efficiency of 0.65. What is this efficiency as a percentage?
questions.explanation: An efficiency of 0.65 is 65% because 0.65 × 100 = 65.
questions.senNote: Checks: Convert decimal efficiency into a percentage.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Identify the useful output transfer of a device.

questions.stem: A blender is designed to spin its blades. Which output is useful?
questions.question: A blender is designed to spin its blades. Which output is useful?
options: Energy transferred to the kinetic store of the blades.
Energy transferred by sound to the surroundings.
Energy transferred to warm the motor casing.
Energy transferred to heat the air around the blender.
questions.correctAnswer: Energy transferred to the kinetic store of the blades.
questions.explanation: The useful output is the transfer that matches the device purpose: spinning the blades.

distractorRationales.text: Energy transferred by sound to the surroundings.
Energy transferred to warm the motor casing.
Energy transferred to heat the air around the blender.

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the useful output transfer of a device.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Recognise wasted transfers in a working device.

questions.stem: A food mixer becomes warm and makes sound while it is working. Which statement is correct?
questions.question: A food mixer becomes warm and makes sound while it is working. Which statement is correct?
options: Some input energy is wasted by heating and sound.
The wasted energy has been destroyed.
The useful output must be sound.
The mixer has no useful energy transfer.
questions.correctAnswer: Some input energy is wasted by heating and sound.
questions.explanation: Wasted transfers are usually dissipated to the surroundings by heating and sound; energy is transferred, not destroyed.

distractorRationales.text: The wasted energy has been destroyed.
The useful output must be sound.
The mixer has no useful energy transfer.

questions.senNote: Checks: Recognise wasted transfers in a working device.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate total input energy from useful output and efficiency.

questions.stem: A device is 50% efficient and transfers 300 J usefully. What was the total input energy transfer?
questions.question: A device is 50% efficient and transfers 300 J usefully. What was the total input energy transfer?
questions.explanation: 50% efficiency means useful output is half the input, so the input is 300 ÷ 0.50 = 600 J.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate total input energy from useful output and efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate useful output energy from input energy and efficiency.

questions.stem: A lamp is 20% efficient. It receives 500 J of energy. How much energy is transferred usefully as light?
questions.question: A lamp is 20% efficient. It receives 500 J of energy. How much energy is transferred usefully as light?
questions.explanation: Useful output = 20% of 500 J = 0.20 × 500 = 100 J.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate useful output energy from input energy and efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate wasted energy from total input and useful output.

questions.stem: A drill receives 250 J and transfers 175 J usefully. How much energy is wasted?
questions.question: A drill receives 250 J and transfers 175 J usefully. How much energy is wasted?
questions.explanation: Wasted energy = total input - useful output = 250 - 175 = 75 J.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate wasted energy from total input and useful output.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Interpret arrow width in a Sankey diagram.

questions.stem: In a Sankey diagram, what does the width of an arrow represent?
questions.question: In a Sankey diagram, what does the width of an arrow represent?
options: The amount of energy transferred.
The speed of the device.
The voltage across the device.
The time taken for the transfer.
questions.correctAnswer: The amount of energy transferred.
questions.explanation: Sankey arrows are drawn wider for larger energy transfers.

distractorRationales.text: The speed of the device.
The voltage across the device.
The time taken for the transfer.

questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret arrow width in a Sankey diagram.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate efficiency from Sankey diagram values.

questions.stem: A Sankey diagram shows 250 J input and 150 J useful output. What is the efficiency?
questions.question: A Sankey diagram shows 250 J input and 150 J useful output. What is the efficiency?
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate efficiency from Sankey diagram values.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the efficiency equation using power values.

questions.stem: Which equation can calculate efficiency using power values?
questions.question: Which equation can calculate efficiency using power values?
options: efficiency = useful output power ÷ total input power
efficiency = total input power ÷ useful output power
efficiency = wasted power × useful output power
efficiency = power × time
questions.correctAnswer: efficiency = useful output power ÷ total input power
questions.explanation: Efficiency can be calculated using useful output power divided by total input power.

distractorRationales.text: efficiency = total input power ÷ useful output power
efficiency = wasted power × useful output power
efficiency = power × time

questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the efficiency equation using power values.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate efficiency from input and output power values.

questions.stem: A motor has an input power of 160 W and a useful output power of 120 W. What is its efficiency?
questions.question: A motor has an input power of 160 W and a useful output power of 120 W. What is its efficiency?
questions.explanation: Efficiency = useful output power ÷ input power = 120 ÷ 160 = 0.75 = 75%.
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate efficiency from input and output power values.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Explain how reducing friction can improve efficiency.

questions.stem: Which change would usually improve the efficiency of a machine with moving parts?
questions.question: Which change would usually improve the efficiency of a machine with moving parts?
options: Reduce friction between moving parts.
Increase unwanted heating in the machine.
Make the useful output smaller for the same input.
Add rougher surfaces between moving parts.
questions.correctAnswer: Reduce friction between moving parts.
questions.explanation: Reducing friction reduces wasted energy transfer by heating, so a greater fraction of the input can be useful.

distractorRationales.text: Increase unwanted heating in the machine.
Make the useful output smaller for the same input.
Add rougher surfaces between moving parts.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how reducing friction can improve efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Explain how insulation can improve efficiency.

questions.stem: A hot-water tank is insulated. How can this improve the efficiency of the heating system?
questions.question: A hot-water tank is insulated. How can this improve the efficiency of the heating system?
options: Less energy is wasted to the surroundings.
More energy is created in the water.
The tank needs no input energy.
The useful output becomes sound.
questions.correctAnswer: Less energy is wasted to the surroundings.
questions.explanation: Insulation reduces unwanted transfer to the surroundings, so more of the input energy remains useful.

distractorRationales.text: More energy is created in the water.
The tank needs no input energy.
The useful output becomes sound.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain how insulation can improve efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Compare decimal and percentage efficiency values.

questions.stem: Device X is 0.55 efficient. Device Y is 48% efficient. Which device is more efficient?
questions.question: Device X is 0.55 efficient. Device Y is 48% efficient. Which device is more efficient?
options: Device X
Device Y
They have the same efficiency.
They cannot be compared because the units are different.
questions.correctAnswer: Device X
questions.explanation: 0.55 is 55%, which is higher than 48%, so Device X is more efficient.

distractorRationales.text: Device Y
They have the same efficiency.
They cannot be compared because the units are different.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare decimal and percentage efficiency values.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why normal devices are not 100% efficient.

questions.stem: Why is the efficiency of a normal device less than 100%?
questions.question: Why is the efficiency of a normal device less than 100%?
options: Some energy is transferred in unwanted ways.
Energy is destroyed inside the device.
The input energy is always zero.
Useful energy cannot leave a device.
questions.correctAnswer: Some energy is transferred in unwanted ways.
questions.explanation: Normal devices waste some energy, usually to the surroundings by heating or sound, so not all input is useful.

distractorRationales.text: Energy is destroyed inside the device.
The input energy is always zero.
Useful energy cannot leave a device.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why normal devices are not 100% efficient.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Use output data to identify the most efficient machine.

questions.stem: Four machines receive the same input energy. Which machine is most efficient?
questions.question: Four machines receive the same input energy. Which machine is most efficient?
options: The machine with the greatest useful output energy.
The machine with the greatest wasted energy.
The machine with the loudest sound output.
The machine with the highest temperature rise in its casing.
questions.correctAnswer: The machine with the greatest useful output energy.
questions.explanation: With the same input energy, the machine with the greatest useful output has the highest efficiency.

distractorRationales.text: The machine with the greatest wasted energy.
The machine with the loudest sound output.
The machine with the highest temperature rise in its casing.

questions.senNote: Checks: Use output data to identify the most efficient machine.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Find wasted percentage from efficiency.

questions.stem: A device is 72% efficient. What percentage of the input energy is wasted?
questions.question: A device is 72% efficient. What percentage of the input energy is wasted?
questions.explanation: If 72% is useful, the remaining 28% is wasted.
questions.senNote: Checks: Find wasted percentage from efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Calculate efficiency and interpret energy transfers.

learningObjective.statement: Compare useful and wasted transfers using Sankey arrow widths.

questions.stem: A Sankey diagram has a wasted output arrow wider than the useful output arrow. What does this show?
questions.question: A Sankey diagram has a wasted output arrow wider than the useful output arrow. What does this show?
options: More energy is wasted than transferred usefully.
The device is 100% efficient.
The input energy is zero.
The useful output is greater than the wasted output.
questions.correctAnswer: More energy is wasted than transferred usefully.
questions.explanation: A wider arrow represents a larger energy transfer, so the wasted transfer is larger than the useful transfer.

distractorRationales.text: The device is 100% efficient.
The input energy is zero.
The useful output is greater than the wasted output.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare useful and wasted transfers using Sankey arrow widths.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Identify renewable energy resources.

questions.stem: Which row contains only renewable energy resources?
questions.question: Which row contains only renewable energy resources?
options: Wind, solar and hydroelectricity.
Coal, oil and natural gas.
Nuclear fuel, coal and oil.
Natural gas, wind and coal.
questions.correctAnswer: Wind, solar and hydroelectricity.
questions.explanation: Wind, solar and hydroelectricity are replenished naturally on a short timescale.

distractorRationales.text: Coal, oil and natural gas.
Nuclear fuel, coal and oil.
Natural gas, wind and coal.

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify renewable energy resources.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Identify non-renewable energy resources.

questions.stem: Which energy resource is non-renewable?
questions.question: Which energy resource is non-renewable?
options: Natural gas.
Wind.
Tidal power.
Solar radiation.
questions.correctAnswer: Natural gas.
questions.explanation: Natural gas is a fossil fuel with a finite supply, so it is non-renewable.

distractorRationales.text: Wind.
Tidal power.
Solar radiation.

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify non-renewable energy resources.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Describe how a fossil-fuel power station generates electricity.

questions.stem: In a coal-fired power station, what is the main purpose of burning coal?
questions.question: In a coal-fired power station, what is the main purpose of burning coal?
options: To heat water and produce steam to turn a turbine.
To make electrons flow directly out of the coal.
To cool the generator so it spins faster.
To store gravitational potential energy in the boiler.
questions.correctAnswer: To heat water and produce steam to turn a turbine.
questions.explanation: Burning coal transfers energy by heating water; the steam turns a turbine connected to a generator.

distractorRationales.text: To make electrons flow directly out of the coal.
To cool the generator so it spins faster.
To store gravitational potential energy in the boiler.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe how a fossil-fuel power station generates electricity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Compare nuclear and fossil-fuel power stations.

questions.stem: A nuclear power station and a gas-fired power station both use turbines. What is the main difference in how the water is heated?
questions.question: A nuclear power station and a gas-fired power station both use turbines. What is the main difference in how the water is heated?
options: A nuclear station uses energy released from nuclear fuel; a gas station burns fuel.
A nuclear station uses wind to heat water; a gas station uses sunlight.
A nuclear station does not need a generator; a gas station does.
A nuclear station cools water into steam; a gas station condenses steam into gas.
questions.correctAnswer: A nuclear station uses energy released from nuclear fuel; a gas station burns fuel.
questions.explanation: Both can drive turbines, but nuclear fuel releases energy by nuclear processes rather than by burning a fossil fuel.

distractorRationales.text: A nuclear station uses wind to heat water; a gas station uses sunlight.
A nuclear station does not need a generator; a gas station does.
A nuclear station cools water into steam; a gas station condenses steam into gas.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare nuclear and fossil-fuel power stations.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Describe an environmental impact of fossil-fuel use.

questions.stem: Which environmental impact is linked most directly to burning fossil fuels?
questions.question: Which environmental impact is linked most directly to burning fossil fuels?
options: Carbon dioxide is released into the atmosphere.
No waste products are produced.
The fuel supply is replaced immediately.
The Sun stops transferring energy to Earth.
questions.correctAnswer: Carbon dioxide is released into the atmosphere.
questions.explanation: Burning fossil fuels releases carbon dioxide, which contributes to climate change.

distractorRationales.text: No waste products are produced.
The fuel supply is replaced immediately.
The Sun stops transferring energy to Earth.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe an environmental impact of fossil-fuel use.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Evaluate a disadvantage of nuclear power.

questions.stem: Which statement is a disadvantage of using nuclear fuel for electricity generation?
questions.question: Which statement is a disadvantage of using nuclear fuel for electricity generation?
options: It produces radioactive waste that must be stored safely.
It releases carbon dioxide in the same way as coal when operating.
It can only generate electricity at night.
It is replenished each day by sunlight.
questions.correctAnswer: It produces radioactive waste that must be stored safely.
questions.explanation: Nuclear power stations produce radioactive waste, which needs secure long-term handling and storage.

distractorRationales.text: It releases carbon dioxide in the same way as coal when operating.
It can only generate electricity at night.
It is replenished each day by sunlight.

questions.senNote: Checks: Evaluate a disadvantage of nuclear power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why some renewable resources are intermittent.

questions.stem: Why can the output from a wind farm change during a day?
questions.question: Why can the output from a wind farm change during a day?
options: Wind speed changes, so the turbines do not always transfer energy at the same rate.
Wind turbines use up the wind permanently.
The turbines only work when fossil fuels are burned nearby.
The generator stops because wind has no kinetic energy.
questions.correctAnswer: Wind speed changes, so the turbines do not always transfer energy at the same rate.
questions.explanation: Wind power depends on wind speed, so the electrical output can vary with weather conditions.

distractorRationales.text: Wind turbines use up the wind permanently.
The turbines only work when fossil fuels are burned nearby.
The generator stops because wind has no kinetic energy.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why some renewable resources are intermittent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Apply reliability ideas to solar power.

questions.stem: A house has solar panels but uses most electricity after sunset. What is the main limitation of the panels at that time?
questions.question: A house has solar panels but uses most electricity after sunset. What is the main limitation of the panels at that time?
options: They receive little or no sunlight, so their output is low.
They become non-renewable after one day of use.
They release more carbon dioxide at night.
They can only produce electricity if coal is burned.
questions.correctAnswer: They receive little or no sunlight, so their output is low.
questions.explanation: Solar panels depend on incoming light, so their output is much lower at night.

distractorRationales.text: They become non-renewable after one day of use.
They release more carbon dioxide at night.
They can only produce electricity if coal is burned.

questions.senNote: Checks: Apply reliability ideas to solar power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why tidal power can be predictable.

questions.stem: Tidal power is often described as predictable. Why?
questions.question: Tidal power is often described as predictable. Why?
options: The times of tides can be forecast in advance.
Tides occur only when fossil fuels are burned.
The sea level is always exactly the same.
Tidal stations produce no electricity during every tide.
questions.correctAnswer: The times of tides can be forecast in advance.
questions.explanation: Tides follow regular patterns, so the timing of tidal electricity generation can be predicted.

distractorRationales.text: Tides occur only when fossil fuels are burned.
The sea level is always exactly the same.
Tidal stations produce no electricity during every tide.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why tidal power can be predictable.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Describe the energy transfer in hydroelectric generation.

questions.stem: In a hydroelectric power station, which store decreases as water flows down through the turbines?
questions.question: In a hydroelectric power station, which store decreases as water flows down through the turbines?
options: Gravitational potential energy store.
Chemical energy store of the water.
Nuclear energy store of the water.
Elastic potential energy store of the dam wall only.
questions.correctAnswer: Gravitational potential energy store.
questions.explanation: Water held high in a reservoir has gravitational potential energy that is transferred as it flows down.

distractorRationales.text: Chemical energy store of the water.
Nuclear energy store of the water.
Elastic potential energy store of the dam wall only.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe the energy transfer in hydroelectric generation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Choose an energy resource for changing demand.

questions.stem: A town needs extra electricity for a short evening peak. Which feature makes a gas-fired power station useful for this demand?
questions.question: A town needs extra electricity for a short evening peak. Which feature makes a gas-fired power station useful for this demand?
options: It can increase output relatively quickly.
It produces electricity only when the tide turns.
It has no fuel cost.
It stores all its electricity as chemical energy in the generator.
questions.correctAnswer: It can increase output relatively quickly.
questions.explanation: Gas-fired stations can respond quickly compared with some other large power stations, so they can help meet peak demand.

distractorRationales.text: It produces electricity only when the tide turns.
It has no fuel cost.
It stores all its electricity as chemical energy in the generator.

questions.senNote: Checks: Choose an energy resource for changing demand.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Evaluate nuclear power for base load supply.

questions.stem: Why is a nuclear power station often used to provide a steady base supply of electricity?
questions.question: Why is a nuclear power station often used to provide a steady base supply of electricity?
options: It can operate continuously for long periods.
It starts and stops every few seconds with demand.
It depends directly on whether it is windy.
It produces electricity only in daylight.
questions.correctAnswer: It can operate continuously for long periods.
questions.explanation: Nuclear stations are suited to steady generation over long periods, so they can contribute to base load supply.

distractorRationales.text: It starts and stops every few seconds with demand.
It depends directly on whether it is windy.
It produces electricity only in daylight.

questions.senNote: Checks: Evaluate nuclear power for base load supply.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Select a suitable site for solar generation.

questions.stem: Which site would usually be best for a solar farm?
questions.question: Which site would usually be best for a solar farm?
options: A large open area with high average sunlight.
A deep valley that is shaded all day.
A site that is underwater at high tide.
A coal seam far below ground.
questions.correctAnswer: A large open area with high average sunlight.
questions.explanation: Solar farms need a large area with strong sunlight to generate useful amounts of electricity.

distractorRationales.text: A deep valley that is shaded all day.
A site that is underwater at high tide.
A coal seam far below ground.

questions.senNote: Checks: Select a suitable site for solar generation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Select a suitable site for wind generation.

questions.stem: A company wants to install wind turbines. Which site factor is most important?
questions.question: A company wants to install wind turbines. Which site factor is most important?
options: A consistently high wind speed.
A low number of sunny days.
A nearby supply of uranium.
A river with no change in height.
questions.correctAnswer: A consistently high wind speed.
questions.explanation: Wind turbines need moving air, so a windy site is essential for useful output.

distractorRationales.text: A low number of sunny days.
A nearby supply of uranium.
A river with no change in height.

questions.senNote: Checks: Select a suitable site for wind generation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Select a suitable site for hydroelectric generation.

questions.stem: Which feature is most useful for hydroelectric generation?
questions.question: Which feature is most useful for hydroelectric generation?
options: A large volume of water stored at a height.
A flat desert with no river.
A constant supply of coal.
A panel facing the Sun.
questions.correctAnswer: A large volume of water stored at a height.
questions.explanation: Hydroelectric generation uses water flowing down from a height to turn turbines.

distractorRationales.text: A flat desert with no river.
A constant supply of coal.
A panel facing the Sun.

questions.senNote: Checks: Select a suitable site for hydroelectric generation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why fossil fuels are finite.

questions.stem: Why are fossil fuels described as finite resources?
questions.question: Why are fossil fuels described as finite resources?
options: They are used faster than they are formed.
They are produced by wind every day.
They cannot transfer energy.
They are made when generators spin.
questions.correctAnswer: They are used faster than they are formed.
questions.explanation: Fossil fuels take millions of years to form, so supplies can run out on human timescales.

distractorRationales.text: They are produced by wind every day.
They cannot transfer energy.
They are made when generators spin.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why fossil fuels are finite.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Describe biofuel as a renewable resource.

questions.stem: Which statement about biofuels is correct?
questions.question: Which statement about biofuels is correct?
options: They are made from recently living material and can be replaced by growing more plants.
They are fossil fuels formed from crude oil in a refinery.
They are always more reliable than every other power source.
They contain no stored chemical energy.
questions.correctAnswer: They are made from recently living material and can be replaced by growing more plants.
questions.explanation: Biofuels come from biological material, so new crops can replace the fuel source if managed sustainably.

distractorRationales.text: They are fossil fuels formed from crude oil in a refinery.
They are always more reliable than every other power source.
They contain no stored chemical energy.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe biofuel as a renewable resource.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Identify the source of geothermal energy.

questions.stem: Geothermal power stations use energy transferred from which source?
questions.question: Geothermal power stations use energy transferred from which source?
options: Hot rocks inside Earth.
The chemical store of coal.
The motion of sea waves only.
The elastic store of overhead cables.
questions.correctAnswer: Hot rocks inside Earth.
questions.explanation: Geothermal generation uses thermal energy from hot rocks below Earth’s surface.

distractorRationales.text: The chemical store of coal.
The motion of sea waves only.
The elastic store of overhead cables.

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the source of geothermal energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Describe how wave power can generate electricity.

questions.stem: What causes the useful energy transfer in a wave power device?
questions.question: What causes the useful energy transfer in a wave power device?
options: The movement of waves drives a mechanism connected to a generator.
Uranium atoms burn in seawater.
Coal particles dissolve into the sea.
Sunlight directly heats overhead cables.
questions.correctAnswer: The movement of waves drives a mechanism connected to a generator.
questions.explanation: Wave devices use the motion of waves to drive equipment that generates electricity.

distractorRationales.text: Uranium atoms burn in seawater.
Coal particles dissolve into the sea.
Sunlight directly heats overhead cables.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe how wave power can generate electricity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Evaluate fossil fuels using cost and environmental impact.

questions.stem: A country can build a coal station cheaply but wants to reduce carbon dioxide emissions. Which issue is the best criticism of coal?
questions.question: A country can build a coal station cheaply but wants to reduce carbon dioxide emissions. Which issue is the best criticism of coal?
options: Burning coal releases carbon dioxide during operation.
Coal produces electricity only when the Sun is shining.
Coal is a renewable resource that never runs out.
Coal stations cannot transfer energy to generators.
questions.correctAnswer: Burning coal releases carbon dioxide during operation.
questions.explanation: Coal may be cheap in some cases, but burning it releases carbon dioxide.

distractorRationales.text: Coal produces electricity only when the Sun is shining.
Coal is a renewable resource that never runs out.
Coal stations cannot transfer energy to generators.

questions.senNote: Checks: Evaluate fossil fuels using cost and environmental impact.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Interpret data about environmental impact.

questions.stem: A table shows: coal has high carbon dioxide emissions; wind has very low direct emissions; gas has medium emissions. Which resource best fits a low-carbon target?
questions.question: A table shows: coal has high carbon dioxide emissions; wind has very low direct emissions; gas has medium emissions. Which resource best fits a low-carbon target?
options: Wind.
Coal.
Gas.
Any fossil fuel equally.
questions.correctAnswer: Wind.
questions.explanation: Wind has very low direct carbon dioxide emissions compared with coal and gas.

distractorRationales.text: Coal.
Gas.
Any fossil fuel equally.

questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret data about environmental impact.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Interpret reliability data when choosing a resource.

questions.stem: A table shows: wind output varies with weather; gas output can be increased when needed; solar output is low at night. Which resource is most useful for a sudden evening peak?
questions.question: A table shows: wind output varies with weather; gas output can be increased when needed; solar output is low at night. Which resource is most useful for a sudden evening peak?
options: Gas.
Solar.
Wind only.
No resource can change output.
questions.correctAnswer: Gas.
questions.explanation: Gas-fired stations can increase output relatively quickly, so they can help meet a sudden peak in demand.

distractorRationales.text: Solar.
Wind only.
No resource can change output.

questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret reliability data when choosing a resource.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Evaluate a disadvantage of hydroelectric power.

questions.stem: Which disadvantage can apply to a large hydroelectric scheme?
questions.question: Which disadvantage can apply to a large hydroelectric scheme?
options: Large areas of land may be flooded to make a reservoir.
It releases carbon dioxide by burning coal in the dam.
It cannot generate electricity using turbines.
It uses uranium fuel that becomes radioactive waste.
questions.correctAnswer: Large areas of land may be flooded to make a reservoir.
questions.explanation: Hydroelectric schemes may require reservoirs, which can flood land and affect habitats or communities.

distractorRationales.text: It releases carbon dioxide by burning coal in the dam.
It cannot generate electricity using turbines.
It uses uranium fuel that becomes radioactive waste.

questions.senNote: Checks: Evaluate a disadvantage of hydroelectric power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Evaluate a local disadvantage of wind power.

questions.stem: Which objection is most likely to be made about a wind farm near houses?
questions.question: Which objection is most likely to be made about a wind farm near houses?
options: Noise and visual impact.
Radioactive waste from the turbine blades.
Carbon dioxide from burning wind.
The turbines use up the Sun’s energy.
questions.correctAnswer: Noise and visual impact.
questions.explanation: Wind farms can be criticised for noise and their visual effect on the landscape.

distractorRationales.text: Radioactive waste from the turbine blades.
Carbon dioxide from burning wind.
The turbines use up the Sun’s energy.

questions.senNote: Checks: Evaluate a local disadvantage of wind power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why storage can support intermittent resources.

questions.stem: Why can energy storage be useful when a grid uses a lot of wind and solar power?
questions.question: Why can energy storage be useful when a grid uses a lot of wind and solar power?
options: Their output may not match the times when demand is highest.
Renewable resources cannot ever transfer energy.
Storage turns fossil fuels into uranium.
Demand is always zero when renewable output is high.
questions.correctAnswer: Their output may not match the times when demand is highest.
questions.explanation: Intermittent resources can generate at times that do not match demand, so storage can help balance supply and demand.

distractorRationales.text: Renewable resources cannot ever transfer energy.
Storage turns fossil fuels into uranium.
Demand is always zero when renewable output is high.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why storage can support intermittent resources.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Evaluate why an energy mix may be used.

questions.stem: Why might a country use a mixture of energy resources rather than only one resource?
questions.question: Why might a country use a mixture of energy resources rather than only one resource?
options: Different resources have different costs, reliability and environmental impacts.
Only one resource can ever work at a time.
Renewable resources all release the same waste products as coal.
A generator cannot be connected to more than one type of power station.
questions.correctAnswer: Different resources have different costs, reliability and environmental impacts.
questions.explanation: Using a mix can balance reliability, cost, demand and environmental effects.

distractorRationales.text: Only one resource can ever work at a time.
Renewable resources all release the same waste products as coal.
A generator cannot be connected to more than one type of power station.

questions.senNote: Checks: Evaluate why an energy mix may be used.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Choose a resource for a local context.

questions.stem: A remote island has strong regular tides but importing fuel is expensive. Which resource is most suitable to investigate first?
questions.question: A remote island has strong regular tides but importing fuel is expensive. Which resource is most suitable to investigate first?
options: Tidal power.
Coal-fired generation.
Oil-fired generation only.
A solar farm with no sunlight data.
questions.correctAnswer: Tidal power.
questions.explanation: Regular tides are a local renewable resource, and using them could reduce the need to import fuel.

distractorRationales.text: Coal-fired generation.
Oil-fired generation only.
A solar farm with no sunlight data.

questions.senNote: Checks: Choose a resource for a local context.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Compare fuel supply needs for energy resources.

questions.stem: Which power station type needs a continuing supply of fuel to be delivered?
questions.question: Which power station type needs a continuing supply of fuel to be delivered?
options: Gas-fired power station.
Wind farm.
Solar farm.
Tidal barrage.
questions.correctAnswer: Gas-fired power station.
questions.explanation: A gas-fired station needs fuel to be supplied, whereas wind, solar and tidal resources use naturally available energy transfers.

distractorRationales.text: Wind farm.
Solar farm.
Tidal barrage.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare fuel supply needs for energy resources.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Identify a low direct-emission resource.

questions.stem: Which resource has very low carbon dioxide emissions while generating electricity?
questions.question: Which resource has very low carbon dioxide emissions while generating electricity?
options: Solar power.
Coal.
Oil.
Natural gas.
questions.correctAnswer: Solar power.
questions.explanation: Solar panels do not burn fuel while generating electricity, so direct carbon dioxide emissions are very low.

distractorRationales.text: Coal.
Oil.
Natural gas.

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify a low direct-emission resource.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Match energy resources to their generation method.

questions.stem: Which pairing is correct?
questions.question: Which pairing is correct?
options: Uranium: nuclear power station.
Coal: solar panel.
Wind: fossil-fuel boiler.
Tide: diesel generator fuel.
questions.correctAnswer: Uranium: nuclear power station.
questions.explanation: Uranium is used as nuclear fuel in nuclear power stations.

distractorRationales.text: Coal: solar panel.
Wind: fossil-fuel boiler.
Tide: diesel generator fuel.

questions.senNote: Checks: Match energy resources to their generation method.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Compare environmental issues for coal and nuclear power.

questions.stem: Which comparison between coal and nuclear power is correct?
questions.question: Which comparison between coal and nuclear power is correct?
options: Coal releases carbon dioxide when burned; nuclear power produces radioactive waste.
Coal produces radioactive waste only; nuclear power releases carbon dioxide by burning uranium.
Both are renewable because their fuels are replaced daily.
Neither resource has any waste or environmental issue.
questions.correctAnswer: Coal releases carbon dioxide when burned; nuclear power produces radioactive waste.
questions.explanation: Coal combustion releases carbon dioxide, while nuclear generation creates radioactive waste that must be managed.

distractorRationales.text: Coal produces radioactive waste only; nuclear power releases carbon dioxide by burning uranium.
Both are renewable because their fuels are replaced daily.
Neither resource has any waste or environmental issue.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare environmental issues for coal and nuclear power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Use reliability and operating time when comparing resources.

questions.stem: Two power stations have the same maximum power output. Station A runs steadily most of the day; Station B only runs when the weather is suitable. Which conclusion is most reasonable?
questions.question: Two power stations have the same maximum power output. Station A runs steadily most of the day; Station B only runs when the weather is suitable. Which conclusion is most reasonable?
options: Station A is likely to transfer more energy over the whole day.
Station B must transfer more energy because it is weather-dependent.
Both must transfer exactly the same energy every day.
Maximum power output alone tells the total daily energy transfer.
questions.correctAnswer: Station A is likely to transfer more energy over the whole day.
questions.explanation: Energy transferred depends on power and time, so a station running for longer can transfer more energy over the day.

distractorRationales.text: Station B must transfer more energy because it is weather-dependent.
Both must transfer exactly the same energy every day.
Maximum power output alone tells the total daily energy transfer.

questions.senNote: Checks: Use reliability and operating time when comparing resources.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Explain why supply must be matched to demand.

questions.stem: Why must electricity generation be managed to match demand?
questions.question: Why must electricity generation be managed to match demand?
options: Electricity is difficult to store in large amounts, so supply must closely follow use.
Demand is always the same at every time of day.
Generators stop working if the National Grid is connected.
Renewable resources make demand disappear.
questions.correctAnswer: Electricity is difficult to store in large amounts, so supply must closely follow use.
questions.explanation: Large-scale electricity storage is limited, so generation is adjusted to match changing demand.

distractorRationales.text: Demand is always the same at every time of day.
Generators stop working if the National Grid is connected.
Renewable resources make demand disappear.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain why supply must be matched to demand.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Evaluate public acceptance issues for renewable resources.

questions.stem: A planned wind farm has low operating emissions but local residents object. Which factor is most likely part of the objection?
questions.question: A planned wind farm has low operating emissions but local residents object. Which factor is most likely part of the objection?
options: Its appearance and noise may affect the local area.
It burns coal next to the houses.
It stores radioactive waste under every turbine.
It uses natural gas piped into each blade.
questions.correctAnswer: Its appearance and noise may affect the local area.
questions.explanation: Wind farms can have low emissions during operation but still raise local concerns about noise and appearance.

distractorRationales.text: It burns coal next to the houses.
It stores radioactive waste under every turbine.
It uses natural gas piped into each blade.

questions.senNote: Checks: Evaluate public acceptance issues for renewable resources.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Apply reliability limits to hydroelectric generation.

questions.stem: A hydroelectric station has much lower output during a long dry period. What is the best explanation?
questions.question: A hydroelectric station has much lower output during a long dry period. What is the best explanation?
options: Less water is available to flow through the turbines.
The uranium fuel has run out.
The solar cells have become shaded.
The coal boiler has cooled too much.
questions.correctAnswer: Less water is available to flow through the turbines.
questions.explanation: Hydroelectric output depends on water flow, so dry conditions can reduce the energy available to transfer.

distractorRationales.text: The uranium fuel has run out.
The solar cells have become shaded.
The coal boiler has cooled too much.

questions.senNote: Checks: Apply reliability limits to hydroelectric generation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Evaluate a resource choice from several constraints.

questions.stem: A country has high electricity demand, limited land, and wants low carbon dioxide emissions during operation. Which option is a reasonable part of its energy mix?
questions.question: A country has high electricity demand, limited land, and wants low carbon dioxide emissions during operation. Which option is a reasonable part of its energy mix?
options: Nuclear power.
Only domestic coal fires.
Wind turbines in every shaded valley.
A fossil-fuel station with no exhaust treatment and no fuel supply.
questions.correctAnswer: Nuclear power.
questions.explanation: Nuclear power can provide large steady output with low carbon dioxide emissions during operation, though it has radioactive waste issues.

distractorRationales.text: Only domestic coal fires.
Wind turbines in every shaded valley.
A fossil-fuel station with no exhaust treatment and no fuel supply.

questions.senNote: Checks: Evaluate a resource choice from several constraints.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Evaluate renewable and non-renewable energy resources.

learningObjective.statement: Recognise a balanced evaluation of an energy resource.

questions.stem: Which answer gives the best evaluation of an energy resource?
questions.question: Which answer gives the best evaluation of an energy resource?
options: Solar power has low direct emissions, but output depends on light level and time of day.
Solar power is best because it has no disadvantages at all.
Coal is renewable because it is found underground.
Nuclear power is impossible because generators cannot use turbines.
questions.correctAnswer: Solar power has low direct emissions, but output depends on light level and time of day.
questions.explanation: A good evaluation gives both a benefit and a limitation, rather than pretending a resource has no disadvantages.

distractorRationales.text: Solar power is best because it has no disadvantages at all.
Coal is renewable because it is found underground.
Nuclear power is impossible because generators cannot use turbines.

questions.senNote: Checks: Recognise a balanced evaluation of an energy resource.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the gravitational potential energy equation.

questions.stem: Which equation is used to calculate the change in gravitational potential energy?
questions.question: Which equation is used to calculate the change in gravitational potential energy?
options: change in GPE = mass × gravitational field strength × change in height
change in GPE = mass × speed² ÷ 2
change in GPE = force ÷ area
change in GPE = current × potential difference
questions.correctAnswer: change in GPE = mass × gravitational field strength × change in height
questions.explanation: Gravitational potential energy change is calculated using mass × gravitational field strength × change in height.

distractorRationales.text: change in GPE = mass × speed² ÷ 2
change in GPE = force ÷ area
change in GPE = current × potential difference

questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the gravitational potential energy equation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate gravitational potential energy from mass, field strength and height.

questions.stem: A 3.0 kg box is lifted 2.0 m. The gravitational field strength is 10 N/kg. What is the increase in gravitational potential energy?
questions.question: A 3.0 kg box is lifted 2.0 m. The gravitational field strength is 10 N/kg. What is the increase in gravitational potential energy?
options: 6 J
questions.explanation: Use ΔGPE = mgh = 3.0 × 10 × 2.0 = 60 J.

distractorRationales.text: 6 J

questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate gravitational potential energy from mass, field strength and height.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Rearrange the gravitational potential energy equation to find height.

questions.stem: A 5.0 kg mass gains 250 J of gravitational potential energy. The gravitational field strength is 10 N/kg. How far was it lifted?
questions.question: A 5.0 kg mass gains 250 J of gravitational potential energy. The gravitational field strength is 10 N/kg. How far was it lifted?
questions.explanation: Rearrange ΔGPE = mgh to h = ΔGPE ÷ (mg) = 250 ÷ (5.0 × 10) = 5.0 m.
questions.senNote: Checks: Rearrange the gravitational potential energy equation to find height.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Identify the unit of gravitational potential energy.

questions.stem: Which unit is used for gravitational potential energy?
questions.question: Which unit is used for gravitational potential energy?
options: joule
newton
kilogram
metre per second
questions.correctAnswer: joule
questions.explanation: Gravitational potential energy is an energy, so it is measured in joules.

distractorRationales.text: newton
kilogram
metre per second

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the unit of gravitational potential energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the kinetic energy equation.

questions.stem: Which equation gives the kinetic energy of a moving object?
questions.question: Which equation gives the kinetic energy of a moving object?
options: kinetic energy = 0.5 × mass × speed²
kinetic energy = mass × gravitational field strength × height
kinetic energy = force × area
kinetic energy = mass ÷ speed
questions.correctAnswer: kinetic energy = 0.5 × mass × speed²
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is calculated using 0.5 × mass × speed².

distractorRationales.text: kinetic energy = mass × gravitational field strength × height
kinetic energy = force × area
kinetic energy = mass ÷ speed

questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the kinetic energy equation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate kinetic energy from mass and speed.

questions.stem: A 2.0 kg trolley moves at 3.0 m/s. What is its kinetic energy?
questions.question: A 2.0 kg trolley moves at 3.0 m/s. What is its kinetic energy?
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate kinetic energy from mass and speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Explain the effect of changing speed on kinetic energy.

questions.stem: If the speed of a moving object doubles and its mass stays the same, what happens to its kinetic energy?
questions.question: If the speed of a moving object doubles and its mass stays the same, what happens to its kinetic energy?
options: It becomes four times as large.
It doubles.
It halves.
It stays the same.
questions.correctAnswer: It becomes four times as large.
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy depends on speed squared, so doubling speed makes the kinetic energy four times larger.

distractorRationales.text: It doubles.
It halves.
It stays the same.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain the effect of changing speed on kinetic energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Rearrange the kinetic energy equation to find mass.

questions.stem: A toy car has 16 J of kinetic energy at 4.0 m/s. What is its mass?
questions.question: A toy car has 16 J of kinetic energy at 4.0 m/s. What is its mass?
options: 2.0 kg
1.0 kg
8.0 kg
32 kg
questions.correctAnswer: 2.0 kg
questions.explanation: Rearrange KE = 0.5mv² to m = 2KE ÷ v² = 32 ÷ 16 = 2.0 kg.

distractorRationales.text: 1.0 kg
8.0 kg
32 kg

questions.senNote: Checks: Rearrange the kinetic energy equation to find mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the elastic potential energy equation.

questions.stem: Which equation is used for elastic potential energy stored in a stretched spring?
questions.question: Which equation is used for elastic potential energy stored in a stretched spring?
options: elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant × extension²
elastic potential energy = mass × gravitational field strength × height
elastic potential energy = current × time
elastic potential energy = pressure × volume
questions.correctAnswer: elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant × extension²
questions.explanation: Elastic potential energy in a spring is calculated using 0.5 × spring constant × extension².

distractorRationales.text: elastic potential energy = mass × gravitational field strength × height
elastic potential energy = current × time
elastic potential energy = pressure × volume

questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the elastic potential energy equation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate elastic potential energy from spring constant and extension.

questions.stem: A spring has a spring constant of 80 N/m and is stretched by 0.50 m. How much elastic potential energy is stored?
questions.question: A spring has a spring constant of 80 N/m and is stretched by 0.50 m. How much elastic potential energy is stored?
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate elastic potential energy from spring constant and extension.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Explain the squared relationship in elastic potential energy.

questions.stem: A spring is stretched within its limit of proportionality. If the extension doubles, what happens to the elastic potential energy stored?
questions.question: A spring is stretched within its limit of proportionality. If the extension doubles, what happens to the elastic potential energy stored?
options: It becomes four times as large.
It doubles.
It becomes half as large.
It stays the same.
questions.correctAnswer: It becomes four times as large.
questions.explanation: Elastic potential energy depends on extension squared, so doubling extension makes the energy four times larger.

distractorRationales.text: It doubles.
It becomes half as large.
It stays the same.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain the squared relationship in elastic potential energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Recall the specific heat capacity equation.

questions.stem: Which equation links energy change, mass, specific heat capacity and temperature change?
questions.question: Which equation links energy change, mass, specific heat capacity and temperature change?
options: energy change = mass × specific heat capacity × temperature change
energy change = mass × gravitational field strength × height
energy change = 0.5 × mass × speed²
energy change = force ÷ extension
questions.correctAnswer: energy change = mass × specific heat capacity × temperature change
questions.explanation: Heating calculations use ΔE = mcΔθ, where c is specific heat capacity.

distractorRationales.text: energy change = mass × gravitational field strength × height
energy change = 0.5 × mass × speed²
energy change = force ÷ extension

questions.senNote: Checks: Recall the specific heat capacity equation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate energy transferred using specific heat capacity.

questions.stem: A 0.50 kg block has a specific heat capacity of 900 J/kg °C. It warms by 4.0 °C. What is the energy transferred to it?
questions.question: A 0.50 kg block has a specific heat capacity of 900 J/kg °C. It warms by 4.0 °C. What is the energy transferred to it?
questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate energy transferred using specific heat capacity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Rearrange the specific heat capacity equation to find temperature change.

questions.stem: A 2.0 kg object receives 1000 J of energy. Its specific heat capacity is 250 J/kg °C. What is its temperature rise?
questions.question: A 2.0 kg object receives 1000 J of energy. Its specific heat capacity is 250 J/kg °C. What is its temperature rise?
questions.explanation: Rearrange ΔE = mcΔθ to Δθ = ΔE ÷ (mc) = 1000 ÷ (2.0 × 250) = 2.0 °C.
questions.senNote: Checks: Rearrange the specific heat capacity equation to find temperature change.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Choose the correct energy equation from the quantities given.

questions.stem: A question gives mass, speed and asks for energy. Which equation should be used?
questions.question: A question gives mass, speed and asks for energy. Which equation should be used?
options: kinetic energy = 0.5 × mass × speed²
change in GPE = mass × gravitational field strength × height
energy change = mass × specific heat capacity × temperature change
elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant × extension²
questions.correctAnswer: kinetic energy = 0.5 × mass × speed²
questions.explanation: Mass and speed are the quantities needed for the kinetic energy equation.

distractorRationales.text: change in GPE = mass × gravitational field strength × height
energy change = mass × specific heat capacity × temperature change
elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant × extension²

questions.senNote: Checks: Choose the correct energy equation from the quantities given.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Describe energy transfers for a falling object.

questions.stem: A ball falls from a shelf. Ignoring air resistance, which energy transfer happens as it falls?
questions.question: A ball falls from a shelf. Ignoring air resistance, which energy transfer happens as it falls?
options: Gravitational potential energy decreases and kinetic energy increases.
Kinetic energy decreases and gravitational potential energy increases.
Elastic potential energy increases because the ball is not stretched.
Chemical energy increases because the ball is moving.
questions.correctAnswer: Gravitational potential energy decreases and kinetic energy increases.
questions.explanation: As the ball falls, energy is transferred from its gravitational potential store to its kinetic store.

distractorRationales.text: Kinetic energy decreases and gravitational potential energy increases.
Elastic potential energy increases because the ball is not stretched.
Chemical energy increases because the ball is moving.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe energy transfers for a falling object.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Connect energy transferred by a heater to heating calculations.

questions.stem: A student compares how quickly two metal blocks warm up using the same heater. Which measurement is needed to calculate the energy transferred?
questions.question: A student compares how quickly two metal blocks warm up using the same heater. Which measurement is needed to calculate the energy transferred?
options: The time the heater is on and the heater power.
Only the colour of each block.
Only the final temperature of the room.
The distance from the bench to the floor.
questions.correctAnswer: The time the heater is on and the heater power.
questions.explanation: Energy transferred by a heater can be found from power × time before using heating equations.

distractorRationales.text: Only the colour of each block.
Only the final temperature of the room.
The distance from the bench to the floor.

questions.senNote: Checks: Connect energy transferred by a heater to heating calculations.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Apply conservation of energy to a falling object.

questions.stem: A 1.5 kg object loses 45 J of gravitational potential energy as it falls. Ignoring air resistance, what happens to its kinetic energy?
questions.question: A 1.5 kg object loses 45 J of gravitational potential energy as it falls. Ignoring air resistance, what happens to its kinetic energy?
options: It increases by about 45 J.
It decreases by 45 J.
It stays at zero.
It becomes 1.5 J.
questions.correctAnswer: It increases by about 45 J.
questions.explanation: If air resistance is ignored, the lost gravitational potential energy is transferred to kinetic energy.

distractorRationales.text: It decreases by 45 J.
It stays at zero.
It becomes 1.5 J.

questions.senNote: Checks: Apply conservation of energy to a falling object.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Use gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy equations.

learningObjective.statement: Use correct units for energy calculations.

questions.stem: Several energy equations are used in GCSE Physics. What unit should the calculated energy have in each case?
questions.question: Several energy equations are used in GCSE Physics. What unit should the calculated energy have in each case?
options: J
N/kg
m/s
kg °C
questions.correctAnswer: J
questions.explanation: Gravitational, kinetic, elastic and thermal energy changes are all energies, so their unit is the joule, J.

distractorRationales.text: N/kg
m/s
kg °C

questions.senNote: Checks: Use correct units for energy calculations.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Define internal energy in terms of particle energies.

questions.stem: What is the internal energy of a substance?
questions.question: What is the internal energy of a substance?
options: The total kinetic and potential energy of its particles.
The movement energy of the whole object only.
The gravitational potential energy of the object only.
The energy stored only in chemical bonds between atoms.
questions.correctAnswer: The total kinetic and potential energy of its particles.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

distractorRationales.text: The movement energy of the whole object only.
The gravitational potential energy of the object only.
The energy stored only in chemical bonds between atoms.

questions.senNote: Checks: Define internal energy in terms of particle energies.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Link temperature rise to particle kinetic energy.

questions.stem: A metal block is heated and its temperature rises. What happens to the particles on average?
questions.question: A metal block is heated and its temperature rises. What happens to the particles on average?
options: Their kinetic energy increases.
Their mass increases.
Their charge becomes zero.
Their nuclei disappear.
questions.correctAnswer: Their kinetic energy increases.
questions.explanation: A temperature rise means the particles have a greater average kinetic energy.

distractorRationales.text: Their mass increases.
Their charge becomes zero.
Their nuclei disappear.

questions.senNote: Checks: Link temperature rise to particle kinetic energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Describe energy transfer during cooling.

questions.stem: A hot drink cools down in a room. Which statement describes the energy transfer?
questions.question: A hot drink cools down in a room. Which statement describes the energy transfer?
options: Energy is transferred from the drink to the surroundings.
Energy is created inside the drink as it cools.
Energy is transferred from the colder room to the hotter drink.
The internal energy of the drink must stay constant.
questions.correctAnswer: Energy is transferred from the drink to the surroundings.
questions.explanation: A hot object cools because energy is transferred from it to the cooler surroundings.

distractorRationales.text: Energy is created inside the drink as it cools.
Energy is transferred from the colder room to the hotter drink.
The internal energy of the drink must stay constant.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe energy transfer during cooling.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Explain energy transfer during a change of state.

questions.stem: Ice is melting at its melting point. Energy is transferred to the ice, but its temperature does not rise. What is the energy mainly doing?
questions.question: Ice is melting at its melting point. Energy is transferred to the ice, but its temperature does not rise. What is the energy mainly doing?
options: Increasing the potential energy of the particles as bonds are overcome.
Increasing only the average kinetic energy of the particles.
Destroying particles in the ice.
Reducing the mass of each water molecule to zero.
questions.correctAnswer: Increasing the potential energy of the particles as bonds are overcome.
questions.explanation: During melting, transferred energy changes the arrangement of particles and increases particle potential energy rather than temperature.

distractorRationales.text: Increasing only the average kinetic energy of the particles.
Destroying particles in the ice.
Reducing the mass of each water molecule to zero.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain energy transfer during a change of state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Interpret temperature during boiling.

questions.stem: Water is boiling steadily at 100 °C. More energy is supplied. What happens to the temperature of the water while it boils?
questions.question: Water is boiling steadily at 100 °C. More energy is supplied. What happens to the temperature of the water while it boils?
options: It stays at about 100 °C until the water has changed state.
It rises steadily above 100 °C while liquid water remains.
It falls because boiling uses no energy.
It becomes 0 °C because particles stop moving.
questions.correctAnswer: It stays at about 100 °C until the water has changed state.
questions.explanation: During boiling at constant pressure, energy is used for the change of state, so the temperature stays about constant until boiling is complete.

distractorRationales.text: It rises steadily above 100 °C while liquid water remains.
It falls because boiling uses no energy.
It becomes 0 °C because particles stop moving.

questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret temperature during boiling.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: State the meaning of specific heat capacity.

questions.stem: What does specific heat capacity tell you about a material?
questions.question: What does specific heat capacity tell you about a material?
options: The energy needed to raise 1 kg of the material by 1 °C.
The force needed to stretch 1 m of the material.
The mass needed to make 1 N of weight.
The power needed to melt any sample instantly.
questions.correctAnswer: The energy needed to raise 1 kg of the material by 1 °C.
questions.explanation: Specific heat capacity is the energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg by 1 °C.

distractorRationales.text: The force needed to stretch 1 m of the material.
The mass needed to make 1 N of weight.
The power needed to melt any sample instantly.

questions.senNote: Checks: State the meaning of specific heat capacity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Identify the unit of specific heat capacity.

questions.stem: Which unit is used for specific heat capacity?
questions.question: Which unit is used for specific heat capacity?
options: J/kg °C
J
W
N/m
questions.correctAnswer: J/kg °C
questions.explanation: Specific heat capacity is measured in joules per kilogram per degree Celsius, J/kg °C.

distractorRationales.text: J
W
N/m

questions.senNote: Checks: Identify the unit of specific heat capacity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Compare temperature changes using specific heat capacity.

questions.stem: Two equal masses receive the same energy. Material A has a lower specific heat capacity than material B. Which material has the larger temperature rise?
questions.question: Two equal masses receive the same energy. Material A has a lower specific heat capacity than material B. Which material has the larger temperature rise?
options: Material A.
Material B.
Both must have the same temperature rise.
Neither material can warm up.
questions.correctAnswer: Material A.
questions.explanation: For the same mass and energy transfer, a lower specific heat capacity gives a larger temperature rise.

distractorRationales.text: Material B.
Both must have the same temperature rise.
Neither material can warm up.

questions.senNote: Checks: Compare temperature changes using specific heat capacity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Calculate specific heat capacity from energy, mass and temperature change.

questions.stem: A 0.20 kg metal block is heated by 500 J. Its temperature rises by 5.0 °C. What is its specific heat capacity?
questions.question: A 0.20 kg metal block is heated by 500 J. Its temperature rises by 5.0 °C. What is its specific heat capacity?
options: 500 J/kg °C
100 J/kg °C
2500 J/kg °C
50 J/kg °C
questions.correctAnswer: 500 J/kg °C

distractorRationales.text: 100 J/kg °C
2500 J/kg °C
50 J/kg °C

questions.senNote: Checks: Calculate specific heat capacity from energy, mass and temperature change.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Interpret a heating graph during a change of state.

questions.stem: On a heating graph, a flat section shows a substance changing state. What does the flat section mean?
questions.question: On a heating graph, a flat section shows a substance changing state. What does the flat section mean?
options: Temperature stays constant while energy is still being transferred.
No energy is being transferred to the substance.
Particles have stopped moving completely.
The substance has lost all internal energy.
questions.correctAnswer: Temperature stays constant while energy is still being transferred.
questions.explanation: A flat section during a change of state means temperature is constant even though energy is being transferred.

distractorRationales.text: No energy is being transferred to the substance.
Particles have stopped moving completely.
The substance has lost all internal energy.

questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret a heating graph during a change of state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Link heating to an increase in internal energy.

questions.stem: A sealed gas is heated so its temperature rises. Which store increases most directly?
questions.question: A sealed gas is heated so its temperature rises. Which store increases most directly?
options: The internal energy store of the gas.
The gravitational potential energy store of Earth.
The elastic potential energy store of the container only.
The nuclear energy store of each atom.
questions.correctAnswer: The internal energy store of the gas.
questions.explanation: Heating the gas raises its temperature and increases its internal energy.

distractorRationales.text: The gravitational potential energy store of Earth.
The elastic potential energy store of the container only.
The nuclear energy store of each atom.

questions.senNote: Checks: Link heating to an increase in internal energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Describe particle changes during melting.

questions.stem: During melting, what changes about the particles?
questions.question: During melting, what changes about the particles?
options: Their arrangement changes as bonds are overcome.
They become different atoms.
They stop moving.
Their mass becomes zero.
questions.correctAnswer: Their arrangement changes as bonds are overcome.
questions.explanation: Melting changes the arrangement of particles; the particles themselves are not destroyed or turned into different atoms.

distractorRationales.text: They become different atoms.
They stop moving.
Their mass becomes zero.

questions.senNote: Checks: Describe particle changes during melting.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Explain cooling by evaporation using particle energy.

questions.stem: Sweat evaporates from skin. Why can this cool the skin?
questions.question: Sweat evaporates from skin. Why can this cool the skin?
