options: The highest-energy particles leave the liquid, reducing the average energy of those left behind.
Evaporation creates cold energy that flows into the skin.
Particles gain mass as they leave the skin.
The skin gains thermal energy from the sweat.
questions.correctAnswer: The highest-energy particles leave the liquid, reducing the average energy of those left behind.
questions.explanation: Evaporation removes higher-energy particles from the liquid, so the remaining liquid and skin can cool.

distractorRationales.text: Evaporation creates cold energy that flows into the skin.
Particles gain mass as they leave the skin.
The skin gains thermal energy from the sweat.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain cooling by evaporation using particle energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Explain a control used in a specific heat capacity experiment.

questions.stem: In a specific heat capacity experiment, why should insulation be placed around the block?
questions.question: In a specific heat capacity experiment, why should insulation be placed around the block?
options: To reduce energy transfer from the block to the surroundings.
To increase the mass of the heater.
To make the thermometer read zero.
To stop current flowing in the circuit.
questions.correctAnswer: To reduce energy transfer from the block to the surroundings.
questions.explanation: Insulation reduces unwanted energy transfer to the surroundings, improving the estimate of energy transferred to the block.

distractorRationales.text: To increase the mass of the heater.
To make the thermometer read zero.
To stop current flowing in the circuit.

questions.senNote: Checks: Explain a control used in a specific heat capacity experiment.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Interpret a table involving specific heat capacity.

questions.stem: A table shows equal masses of copper and water receive the same energy. Copper has the lower specific heat capacity. Which result is expected?
questions.question: A table shows equal masses of copper and water receive the same energy. Copper has the lower specific heat capacity. Which result is expected?
options: Copper has the larger temperature rise.
Water has the larger temperature rise because it has higher specific heat capacity.
Both have zero temperature rise.
The material with lower specific heat capacity must have lower mass.
questions.correctAnswer: Copper has the larger temperature rise.
questions.explanation: For equal mass and energy transfer, the lower specific heat capacity material warms more.

distractorRationales.text: Water has the larger temperature rise because it has higher specific heat capacity.
Both have zero temperature rise.
The material with lower specific heat capacity must have lower mass.

questions.senNote: Checks: Interpret a table involving specific heat capacity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Distinguish heating from change-of-state energy transfer.

questions.stem: Which observation shows that energy is being used for a change of state rather than raising temperature?
questions.question: Which observation shows that energy is being used for a change of state rather than raising temperature?
options: The temperature stays constant while the substance melts.
The temperature rises steadily in the solid.
The mass of the sample doubles.
The thermometer is removed before heating starts.
questions.correctAnswer: The temperature stays constant while the substance melts.
questions.explanation: During a change of state, energy changes particle arrangement while temperature remains constant.

distractorRationales.text: The temperature rises steadily in the solid.
The mass of the sample doubles.
The thermometer is removed before heating starts.

questions.senNote: Checks: Distinguish heating from change-of-state energy transfer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Relate temperature to average particle speed.

questions.stem: A gas thermometer shows a higher temperature. What has happened to the average speed of the gas particles?
questions.question: A gas thermometer shows a higher temperature. What has happened to the average speed of the gas particles?
options: It has increased.
It has decreased to zero.
It is unchanged because gases have no particles.
It has changed into gravitational field strength.
questions.correctAnswer: It has increased.
questions.explanation: Higher temperature means greater average kinetic energy, so the particles move faster on average.

distractorRationales.text: It has decreased to zero.
It is unchanged because gases have no particles.
It has changed into gravitational field strength.

questions.senNote: Checks: Relate temperature to average particle speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Distinguish temperature from total internal energy.

questions.stem: A large beaker and a small beaker contain water at the same temperature. Which statement is correct?
questions.question: A large beaker and a small beaker contain water at the same temperature. Which statement is correct?
options: The larger beaker can contain more internal energy because it has more particles.
They must contain exactly the same internal energy because their temperatures are equal.
The smaller beaker must contain more internal energy because it has less water.
Neither beaker contains internal energy.
questions.correctAnswer: The larger beaker can contain more internal energy because it has more particles.
questions.explanation: Temperature is linked to average particle kinetic energy, but total internal energy also depends on the amount of substance.

distractorRationales.text: They must contain exactly the same internal energy because their temperatures are equal.
The smaller beaker must contain more internal energy because it has less water.
Neither beaker contains internal energy.

questions.senNote: Checks: Distinguish temperature from total internal energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.specStatement: Describe internal energy and energy stores.

learningObjective.statement: Use correct units in specific heat capacity calculations.

questions.stem: A 500 g sample receives 2100 J and warms by 10 °C. Its specific heat capacity is 420 J/kg °C. Which conversion is needed before using the equation?
questions.question: A 500 g sample receives 2100 J and warms by 10 °C. Its specific heat capacity is 420 J/kg °C. Which conversion is needed before using the equation?
options: 500 g must be written as 0.50 kg.
10 °C must be written as 10 kg.
2100 J must be written as 2100 W.
420 J/kg °C must be written as 420 N/kg.
questions.correctAnswer: 500 g must be written as 0.50 kg.
questions.explanation: The equation uses mass in kilograms, so 500 g should be converted to 0.50 kg.

distractorRationales.text: 10 °C must be written as 10 kg.
2100 J must be written as 2100 W.
420 J/kg °C must be written as 420 N/kg.

questions.senNote: Checks: Use correct units in specific heat capacity calculations.
